notes: Mean and median agree in direction. Node-size shortcut confirmed: removing the node-size visual cue significantly worsens vertex prediction for the large majority of graphs (33/40).

MEAN IS MISLEADING: mean edge error appears to improve (-14.375) but 35/40 graphs got worse and median error nearly 6x'd (3.5 to 20.0). The apparent mean improvement is driven entirely by 2 extreme outliers (syn_dense_large_0069: delta -1006; syn_dense_large_0055: delta -280), both dense+large graphs also flagged as the worst Part 1 spot-check failure. Wilcoxon p is significant in the WORSENING direction (rank-based, correctly detects the 35/40 majority pattern) -- report median/win-loss counts, not the mean, for this comparison.
Median delta = 0, near-even win/loss split (17 worse / 16 better / 7 tied). No statistically significant layout effect on vertex prediction.

Median delta = +1.0 (~negligible), near-even win/loss split (18 worse / 16 better / 6 tied). The -18.65 mean delta is driven almost entirely by one outlier (syn_dense_large_0069: delta -512). Clean null result: model is NOT meaningfully sensitive to sfdp vs Kamada-Kawai layout -- good news for Limitations section, contradicts what raw mean MAE alone would have suggested.

'Flagged' = dense_bucket OR num_nodes>100. Median error is LOWER for flagged (1.0 vs 3.0) despite higher mean -- aggregate is confounded by mixing OOD-size (genuinely high error) with dense-bucket (often low error) into one flag category. See binned rows below for the deconfounded picture.
Same confound as vertex row above -- median again lower for flagged. Deconfounding by size bin (below) is required to interpret this correctly.
Dense safer than sparse at this size for vertices.
Dense already worse for edges at this size -- crossover starts early.
Dense much safer for vertices even at largest in-distribution size.

Dense edge error now ~3.3x sparse (median) -- complete reversal from the 0-15 size bin where they were tied. This is the full-population confirmation of the Part 1 spot-check 'dense+large is catastrophic' finding.
